title: Well Hydraulics in Wedge-Shaped Aquifer: Unsteady Darcian Flow Model Revisited by Lagging Theory

research.question: How do flux and storage lags alter pumping drawdown and stream depletion in a wedge-shaped confined aquifer bounded by two streams or two barriers?
research.system: A point-pumped confined aquifer occupying a radial wedge, with either constant-head stream boundaries or no-flow barrier boundaries.
research.inputData: Wedge angle, pumping-well location and rate, transmissivity, storativity, flux-lag time, storage-lag time, and stream or barrier boundary type.
research.method: Introduce temporal lags into Darcy flux and storage response, solve the point-source wedge problem by successive Laplace, finite Fourier, and Hankel transforms, then calculate drawdown, stream depletion, sensitivities, and first-order variance contributions.
research.benchmark: With lagging effects removed, the analytical solution reduces to the existing Darcian wedge-aquifer model; infinite-domain curves are also used as references for boundary effects.
research.uncertainty: A multivariate first-order variance approximation apportions drawdown uncertainty, but lag parameters lack direct field determination and the summation-integral solution is not suited to parameter estimation.
research.conclusion: Flux lag produces inertial wave-like drawdown and can drive stream depletion above unity, whereas storage lag produces delayed dual-porosity-like response; wedge boundaries control how these effects reach streams or barriers.

narrative.throughline: Place a pumping well inside an alluvial-fan wedge and follow delayed flux and storage responses as they reflect from streams or barriers and reshape drawdown and stream capture.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: Classical wedge-aquifer solutions combine Darcy flow with radial boundary geometry.
narrative.stakes: At high flow velocity or with secondary-pore exchange, simultaneous Darcy flux and storage response can miss transient inertia and structural delay.
narrative.resolution: Two lag times enter the governing law, survive an exact transform sequence, and separate inertial from microstructural signatures in drawdown and stream depletion.

background.premise: Alluvial aquifers can occupy sector-shaped domains bounded radially by streams.
background.visibleEvidence: Two streams form the sides of a fan-shaped confined aquifer with a pumping well inside.
background.whyNow: Wedge angle and boundary type determine how pumping disturbance reaches surface-water boundaries.
background.boundary: The analytical geometry idealizes boundaries as straight radial streams or barriers extending from the wedge apex.
background.sourceExcerpt: streams in radial directions with different angles, displaying a sector
background.motionPurpose: Establish the fan geometry that channels the pumping response.
background.stepDetail: Open two radial boundaries around a pumping well and label their included angle.
background.transitionBridge: Inside this geometry, the constitutive timing becomes important when flow accelerates.

background.premise: Moderately high pumping-induced velocity can introduce an inertial effect that classical laminar Darcy response does not represent.
background.visibleEvidence: A rapid inward flux arrow lags behind the steepening hydraulic-gradient arrow near the well.
background.whyNow: The mismatch can create transient drawdown and stream-depletion behavior absent from a simultaneous-response model.
background.boundary: The paper represents inertia through a flux time lag rather than a full nonlinear momentum equation.
background.sourceExcerpt: arise when the flow velocity is moderately high due to a high pumping
background.motionPurpose: Separate the onset of hydraulic gradient from the onset of flow.
background.stepDetail: Advance the gradient arrow first and delay the inward flux arrow.
background.transitionBridge: A second lag represents water exchange with poorly connected pore space.

background.premise: Flux and storage lag parameters represent high-speed inertia and dead-end-pore structural interaction as distinct mechanisms.
background.visibleEvidence: One clock sits on the flowing pore network and another on side-pocket storage pores.
background.whyNow: Distinct lag controls permit the model to separate wave-like inertia from delayed storage release.
background.boundary: The lag parameters are phenomenological time scales and are not directly measured in the study.
background.sourceExcerpt: parameters separately represent the effects of inertial force due to high flow speed and dead-end pores due to structural interaction
background.motionPurpose: Assign the two delays to physically different aquifer processes.
background.stepDetail: Attach tau_q to the main flow path and tau_h to side-pore exchange.
background.transitionBridge: The paired clocks are embedded in the constitutive flux law.

methods.validity: The lagging relation follows the paper's dual-phase lag formulation and stated first-order approximation.

methods.stepDetail: Place q at one delayed time and the head gradient at the other.

methods.validity: The approximation retains the Taylor terms used in the derivation and is tied to that truncation.

methods.motionPurpose: Replace shifted clocks with local time-derivative terms.
methods.stepDetail: Collapse each delayed symbol into its retained Taylor-series contribution.
methods.transitionBridge: The constitutive equation is inserted into the pumped wedge balance.

methods.input: Pumping rate and well coordinates inside the wedge
methods.output: Governing equation with a Dirac pumping term
methods.validity: The well is idealized as a point source in the two-dimensional wedge domain.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Section 1 Introduction; formulated in Section 2.1
methods.sourceExcerpt: The withdrawal of water from the aquifer is formulated by a point source
methods.motionPurpose: Focus radial drawdown on the specified well coordinate.
methods.stepDetail: Place a pulsing extraction marker at radius r0 and angle theta0.
methods.transitionBridge: The resulting time-space equation is separated by successive transforms.

methods.validity: The transform follows the initial conditions and Laplace parameter definitions in the derivation.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.2 Drawdown solution for stream-stream wedge, Equation 17
methods.sourceExcerpt: As a first step to derive the drawdown solution
methods.visualObject: Time axis folding into Laplace parameter p
methods.motionPurpose: Remove explicit time dependence from the wedge equation.
methods.stepDetail: Fold the transient drawdown timeline into a Laplace-domain field.

methods.input: Laplace-domain wedge equation with stream boundaries
methods.output: Fourier sine modes satisfying stream-side conditions

methods.sourceExcerpt: Next, the finite Fourier sine transform eliminates the
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve boundary-compatible variation across the wedge angle.
methods.stepDetail: Decompose the angular drawdown shape into sine modes between the streams.
methods.transitionBridge: Each angular mode retains a radial equation.

methods.output: Algebraic drawdown in the Hankel domain
methods.validity: The Hankel order follows the angular eigenvalue and the unbounded radial extent assumed by the model.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Finally, the Hankel transform method removes the
methods.motionPurpose: Convert radial derivatives into Hankel-domain mode amplitudes.
methods.stepDetail: Replace radial drawdown profiles with their Bessel-weighted spectrum.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved spectrum enters the first inverse operation in M07.

methods.output: Time-dependent drawdown in Fourier sine-Hankel space

methods.motionPurpose: Restore time without yet restoring radius or angle.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the source inverse-Laplace relation and retain every Fourier-Hankel mode.
methods.transitionBridge: The time-dependent spectrum enters the inverse Hankel operation in M08.

methods.input: Time-dependent drawdown in Fourier sine-Hankel space
methods.output: Time-dependent radial drawdown for every Fourier sine mode
methods.validity: Equation 26 applies the inverse Hankel transform of the order set by each angular mode.
methods.sourceExcerpt: By defining the joint inverse of Fourier sine-Hankel transforms as
methods.visualObject: Hankel spectrum expanding into radial Bessel profiles
methods.motionPurpose: Restore radius while retaining the angular Fourier modes.
methods.stepDetail: Integrate each Hankel spectrum against its Bessel kernel and preserve its Fourier-mode label.
methods.transitionBridge: The radial mode profiles enter the inverse Fourier operation in M09.

methods.input: Time-dependent radial drawdown for all Fourier sine modes
methods.output: Transient physical-space drawdown in radius angle and time
methods.validity: Equation 28 sums the inverse finite-Fourier sine modes after the inverse Laplace and Hankel operations.
methods.sourceExcerpt: one obtains the drawdown in physical space:
methods.visualObject: Radial sine modes assembling into a full wedge drawdown surface
methods.motionPurpose: Restore the angular coordinate and complete the three-stage inverse sequence.
methods.stepDetail: Sum the radial mode profiles over the finite sine basis until the physical wedge field appears.
methods.transitionBridge: The stream solution is reconfigured for no-flow sides by the cosine transform in M10.

methods.input: No-flow conditions on both radial sides of the wedge
methods.output: Fourier cosine-Hankel drawdown solution for the barrier-barrier wedge
methods.validity: The barrier case replaces the stream-case sine functions with cosine functions to satisfy zero normal flux.
methods.sourceExcerpt: but now with Fourier cosine transform
methods.visualObject: No-flow wedge sides generating cosine angular modes
methods.motionPurpose: Map the stream derivation onto the zero-flux barrier geometry.
methods.stepDetail: Replace the sine basis with the source-defined cosine basis and reconstruct the barrier drawdown field.
methods.transitionBridge: The completed stream field supplies boundary-normal volumetric fluxes in M11.

methods.input: Physical stream-stream drawdown and its normal derivatives at both stream sides

methods.output: Individual and total stream depletion ratios
methods.validity: SDR divides each stream volumetric flow rate by pumping rate and sums the two ratios for total depletion.

methods.motionPurpose: Normalize the produced stream fluxes against imposed extraction.
methods.stepDetail: Divide each owned stream-flow history by Q and add the two source-defined SDR curves.
methods.transitionBridge: The drawdown solution is reduced to its zero-lag benchmark in M13.

methods.input: Transformed stream-stream solution with both lag times set to zero
methods.output: The classical Darcian wedge solution of Chan et al. 1978
methods.validity: Equation 24 reduces to Equation 29 when both lag effects vanish, and the paper identifies that expression with the published classical solution.

methods.motionPurpose: Expose the classical-Darcy limit as a direct analytical benchmark.
methods.stepDetail: Set both lag clocks to zero and overlay Equation 29 on the Chan et al. reference curve.
methods.transitionBridge: The benchmarked transient solution becomes the output perturbed for sensitivity in M14.

methods.input: Matched stream-stream and barrier-barrier wedge configurations

methods.input: Modeled time-drawdown response under storage-lag-dominant parameter ordering

return.visualObject: Original stream-bounded aquifer wedge with pumping cone, lag clocks, wave-like drawdown trace, and SDR gauge crossing one
return.claim: In the modeled wedge, dominant flux lag creates wave-like drawdown and can raise SDR above unity through inertial recharge, while dominant storage lag produces an unconfined- or dual-porosity-like delay.
return.claimBoundary: The lag times are not independently established by field data in this study, and the analytical summation-integral form is presented for screening and benchmarking rather than direct parameter estimation.
return.locator: PDF page 1, Abstract; interpretation boundary on PDF page 9, Section 4 Conclusions and limitations
return.sourceExcerpt: drawdown and SDR curves will behave like waves when the flux lagging dominates. The SDR value can be over unit due to the additional recharge driven by inertial force

visual.accentObject: Flux and storage lag clocks on stream or barrier boundaries
visual.geometryNotes: Use polar radius-angle coordinates inside a variable-angle sector; swap constant-head stream sides for no-flow barriers, show the well as a point source, and reconstruct the drawdown field from temporal, angular, and radial transform modes.
